h1Title: Burkitt Lymphoma: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Burkitt lymphoma is one of the fastest-growing human cancers, with a tumor doubling time of approximately 24-48 hours, caused by the constitutive activation of the MYC proto-oncogene through a chromosomal translocation most commonly involving chromosomes 8 and 14. Three clinical variants are recognized — endemic (African), sporadic (non-endemic), and immunodeficiency-associated — each with a different epidemiology but identical molecular pathology. Burkitt lymphoma accounts for roughly 30-50% of all childhood lymphomas in sub-Saharan Africa and approximately 1-2% of adult lymphomas in high-income countries. Despite its extreme proliferative rate, Burkitt lymphoma is highly curable with intensive short-course chemotherapy: overall survival exceeds 90% in children treated in high-resource settings and 50-70% in adults. Rituximab added to chemotherapy backbones significantly improved adult outcomes over the past decade.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1,200-1,500 new cases per year in the United States (SEER 2021); 3-5 per million per year in high-income countries; up to 10 per 100,000 children per year in holoendemic malaria areas of sub-Saharan Africa
keyStats.demographics: Endemic variant: peaks at age 5-7 years in East Africa, male:female ratio approximately 2:1. Sporadic variant: median age 11 years in children, 30-40 years in adults. Immunodeficiency-associated: median age 30-40 years in HIV-positive patients
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis: children 5-10 years (endemic); 30-40 years (sporadic adults); all ages affected
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 5,000-6,000 cases annually in sub-Saharan Africa in children alone; global burden substantially under-reported

definition: Burkitt lymphoma (ICD-10: C83.7) is a highly aggressive mature B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma defined by a t(8;14)(q24;q32) chromosomal translocation — or its variants t(2;8) or t(8;22) — that places the MYC oncogene under the constitutive control of immunoglobulin gene enhancers. Unregulated MYC drives near-universal S-phase entry of all neoplastic cells, producing the characteristic 100% Ki-67 proliferation index on histology and the fastest tumor growth rate of any human cancer. The tumor cells are medium-sized B-lymphocytes with basophilic cytoplasm, multiple small nucleoli, and abundant apoptotic bodies surrounded by macrophages that produce the pathognomonic 'starry sky' appearance under microscopy. The WHO 2022 Classification of Lymphoid Tumors recognizes three subtypes: endemic Burkitt lymphoma (associated with Epstein-Barr virus in >95% of cases in holoendemic malaria regions); sporadic Burkitt lymphoma (EBV in 15-30%); and immunodeficiency-associated Burkitt lymphoma (HIV-positive patients and organ-transplant recipients). All three share identical molecular pathology (MYC translocation, MYC overexpression) but differ in anatomical presentation, EBV association, and clinical setting. Medical oncologists and hematologists manage Burkitt lymphoma; pediatric oncologists manage childhood cases.

typesClassification.description: The most common form worldwide by absolute numbers. EBV is present in virtually all tumors (>95%). Characteristically presents in school-age children (peak age 5-7 years) as a rapidly enlarging jaw (mandibular) or orbital tumor. Holoendemic malaria belt of sub-Saharan Africa and Papua New Guinea are the primary geographic foci. MYC translocation is universal.

Occurs outside the endemic zone; EBV in 15-30% of cases. Predominantly involves the ileocecal region of the bowel as an abdominal mass causing obstruction, intussusception, or perforation. Also involves the ovaries, kidneys, and retroperitoneum. Accounts for 1-2% of adult lymphomas in high-income countries.

Seen in HIV-positive patients (often at relatively preserved CD4 counts of 200-500), organ-transplant recipients, and patients with primary immunodeficiency. Often presents with nodal and extranodal disease. EBV present in 25-40% of HIV-associated cases. Outcomes improved substantially with cART and rituximab-containing regimens.

primarySymptoms: Rapidly enlarging jaw or facial mass in the endemic variant — most often involving the posterior mandible adjacent to erupting molar teeth — that doubles in size visibly within days.
Abdominal mass, often ileocecal, causing colicky pain, vomiting, and features of bowel obstruction or acute abdomen in the sporadic variant.
Abdominal distension from massive hepatosplenomegaly, ascites, or bulky retroperitoneal adenopathy.
Orbital or periorbital swelling from a rapidly growing orbital tumor, sometimes with proptosis and reduced vision in the endemic variant.
B symptoms — drenching night sweats, unintentional weight loss exceeding 10% of body weight in 6 months, and fever above 38.5°C — in approximately 30-40% of patients.
Central nervous system involvement at diagnosis in 13-17% of patients: cranial nerve palsies, meningeal disease causing headache and nausea, or spinal cord compression.
Bone marrow involvement (stage IV disease) in 20-30% of cases, presenting with anemia, thrombocytopenia, petechiae, or features resembling acute leukemia.
Peripheral blood circulating lymphoma cells (Burkitt leukemia / L3-ALL) in patients with extensive marrow disease — high white cell count with blast morphology.
Tonsil or oropharyngeal mass causing dysphagia or airway compromise in the sporadic variant, particularly in adults.
earlyWarningSigns: A jaw swelling in an African child growing visibly over days — the speed of growth is characteristic; dental infection does not double in size within 48 hours.
Unexplained abdominal pain with a palpable right lower quadrant mass in a child or adolescent — should prompt urgent imaging before assuming appendicitis.
Rapidly enlarging cervical lymph nodes in an HIV-positive adult, especially with constitutional symptoms — biopsy before assuming reactive lymphadenopathy.
New cranial nerve palsy (facial, oculomotor, abducens) in a child or young adult without neurological history — CNS lymphoma is on the differential.
emergencySigns: Acute abdomen with peritoneal signs from bowel perforation by an abdominal Burkitt lymphoma mass — requires urgent surgical and oncological assessment.

Tumor lysis syndrome at presentation (rare before treatment but possible with extreme tumor burden): hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, and acute kidney injury — a metabolic emergency requiring IV hydration and rasburicase.
Respiratory distress from mediastinal involvement or pleural effusion compressing the airway — rare in Burkitt compared with T-cell lymphoblastic lymphoma but requires urgent evaluation.
Acute paraplegia or severe headache with meningism — spinal cord compression or meningeal involvement requiring emergency MRI and intrathecal treatment.

causes.description: The defining molecular event in all Burkitt lymphoma subtypes. The MYC gene on chromosome 8 is translocated adjacent to the IgH enhancer on chromosome 14, causing constitutive MYC expression that drives unregulated cell-cycle entry, proliferation, and resistance to apoptosis. Variant translocations t(2;8) and t(8;22) place MYC under kappa and lambda light-chain enhancers respectively.

causes.cause: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection and immune evasion
causes.description: EBV is detected in >95% of endemic Burkitt tumors. EBV promotes B-cell immortalization through latency-I gene expression (EBNA-1) and upregulates AID (activation-induced cytidine deaminase), which introduces off-target DNA double-strand breaks that favor the MYC translocation event. In the sporadic and immunodeficiency variants, EBV is present in fewer cases but may still facilitate molecular events.

Chronic malaria drives extreme polyclonal B-cell activation through repeated antigenic stimulation and impairs EBV-specific T-cell surveillance, creating conditions in which EBV-infected B cells can accumulate MYC mutations and undergo malignant transformation. The geographic distribution of endemic Burkitt lymphoma corresponds precisely to the holoendemic malaria belt.

HIV causes CD4 T-cell depletion, chronic B-cell dysregulation, and impaired immunosurveillance of EBV-infected cells, increasing Burkitt lymphoma risk approximately 200-1,000-fold. Burkitt lymphoma in HIV often arises at relatively preserved CD4 counts (>200 cells/µL), unlike other AIDS-defining lymphomas that occur with severe immunodeficiency.

causes.cause: Acquired genomic instability and additional mutations
causes.description: Beyond MYC translocation, Burkitt lymphoma typically harbors additional somatic mutations in ID3, TCF3, and CCND3 that drive autonomous cell proliferation and block differentiation. These co-mutations are identified by next-generation sequencing and are increasingly used in diagnosis and therapeutic stratification.

riskFactors.factor: Childhood residence in holoendemic malaria belt
riskFactors.description: Sub-Saharan Africa (Uganda, Kenya, Tanzania, Nigeria, Ghana) and Papua New Guinea have the highest incidence of endemic Burkitt lymphoma. Malaria-belt geography is a stronger predictor of endemic disease than any individual genetic factor.

HIV raises Burkitt lymphoma risk 200-1,000-fold above the general population. Combination antiretroviral therapy (cART) reduces risk by restoring immune surveillance; Burkitt lymphoma can still occur at high CD4 counts.
Male:female ratio is approximately 2:1 in endemic disease. The male excess in sporadic disease is less pronounced and may relate to differences in immune regulatory biology.

riskFactors.factor: Age 5-15 years (endemic) or any age in immunodeficiency-associated disease
riskFactors.description: Endemic disease peaks sharply at age 5-7 years. Sporadic adult disease has no strong age peak but is more common in young adults. Immunodeficiency-associated disease can occur at any adult age.

X-linked agammaglobulinemia, common variable immunodeficiency, and Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome carry increased risk of Burkitt lymphoma through failure of normal B-cell homeostasis and impaired viral control.

Post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (PTLD) includes Burkitt-type lymphoma in approximately 5% of PTLD cases, particularly in EBV-mismatch transplants and those on high-intensity calcineurin inhibitor regimens.

affectedDemographics: Sub-Saharan African children aged 5-10 years represent the highest absolute disease burden globally; endemic Burkitt lymphoma accounts for 30-50% of all childhood lymphomas in the malaria belt.

In high-income countries, sporadic Burkitt lymphoma affects children and adolescents disproportionately — it is the most common pediatric lymphoma after Hodgkin lymphoma and T-cell lymphoblastic lymphoma.
HIV-positive adults, particularly those not on cART or with incomplete viral suppression, carry a 200-1,000-fold elevated risk at any CD4 count.
Adult sporadic Burkitt lymphoma is uncommon but important: median age 30-40 years in most series; outcomes in adults are substantially worse than in children (OS 50-70% vs >90%).
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis requires tissue biopsy. The characteristic histopathological appearance — medium B-cells with 100% Ki-67 proliferative index and a starry sky pattern from interspersed macrophages — is highly distinctive. The WHO 2022 classification defines Burkitt lymphoma by: (1) morphology; (2) immunophenotype (CD20+, CD10+, BCL6+, BCL2 negative, TdT negative); (3) MYC translocation confirmed by FISH; and (4) absence of double-hit (concurrent MYC and BCL2/BCL6 rearrangements), which defines a separate entity. Cytogenetics/FISH for t(8;14) and absence of BCL2/BCL6 rearrangements is mandatory to distinguish Burkitt lymphoma from double-hit high-grade B-cell lymphoma, as they require different chemotherapy. After diagnosis, staging by CT-PET of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis, bone marrow trephine biopsy, and lumbar puncture for CSF cytology and flow cytometry documents disease extent. The Murphy/St Jude staging system is used for children; the Ann Arbor system for adults. Laboratory workup includes lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) — markedly elevated in almost all cases and a key prognostic marker — uric acid, phosphate, potassium, and creatinine to detect pre-treatment tumor lysis syndrome, and HIV and hepatitis B serology.

diagnosticTests.test: Surgical core-needle or open biopsy with flow cytometry and immunohistochemistry
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides tissue for morphological diagnosis, immunophenotyping (CD20, CD10, BCL6, BCL2, TdT, Ki-67), and molecular testing — mandatory before treatment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Core-needle biopsy under local anaesthesia (20-30 minutes) or open surgical biopsy under general anaesthesia. Tissue is processed by the pathology lab over 3-7 days for full immunohistochemistry and molecular studies.

diagnosticTests.test: FISH for MYC, BCL2, BCL6 rearrangements
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms MYC translocation and excludes double-hit (BCL2/BCL6 co-rearrangement) high-grade B-cell lymphoma, which has a worse prognosis and needs different treatment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on the biopsy tissue in the molecular pathology lab. Results in 3-5 days. A result showing isolated MYC rearrangement without BCL2/BCL6 confirms Burkitt lymphoma.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Stages disease by identifying all nodal and extranodal tumor sites including bone marrow, CNS, and occult sites; establishes baseline for treatment response assessment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Radiotracer injection 60 minutes before a 20-minute scan in the scanner. Mildly radioactive; patients should minimize prolonged close contact with small children for a few hours after.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents stage IV disease (marrow involvement); identifies Burkitt leukemia transformation in extensive disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small sample taken under local anaesthesia from the posterior iliac crest; a brief, dull, aching procedure taking 10-15 minutes. Results in 3-5 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Lumbar puncture with CSF cytology and flow cytometry
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for CNS involvement at diagnosis; performed in all patients regardless of neurological symptoms, as treatment differs with CNS disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies on side; local anaesthetic; needle placed between lumbar vertebrae to collect CSF. Results in 24-48 hours. Therapeutic intrathecal chemotherapy is given at the same time if CNS involvement is present.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum LDH, uric acid, phosphate, potassium, creatinine
diagnosticTests.purpose: LDH quantifies tumor burden and prognosis; uric acid and electrolytes detect pre-existing or impending tumor lysis syndrome requiring prophylaxis before starting chemotherapy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results within hours. Markedly elevated LDH (often 5-10× normal) is characteristic of Burkitt lymphoma at presentation.

treatmentOverview: Burkitt lymphoma demands immediate, intensive, short-course chemotherapy — delay of more than 24-48 hours from diagnosis to treatment initiation is associated with significantly worse outcomes given the extreme proliferative rate. Two internationally used regimens dominate: CODOX-M/IVAC (cyclophosphamide, vincristine, doxorubicin, high-dose methotrexate / ifosfamide, etoposide, cytarabine) as published by Magrath et al. and refined by Mead et al. (Leuk Lymphoma 2008), and the DA-EPOCH-R (dose-adjusted etoposide, prednisone, vincristine, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin plus rituximab) favored in the US for adults, and the French LMB (Lymphomes Malins B) pediatric protocol used across Europe and Africa. Rituximab (anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody) is added to all adult regimens and increasingly to pediatric regimens outside resource-limited settings; it improves 2-year overall survival in adults from approximately 50-60% to 65-75% in the HOVON 97 and other prospective studies. CNS prophylaxis with intrathecal methotrexate and cytarabine is mandatory in all patients regardless of CNS status at diagnosis; high-dose systemic methotrexate achieves CNS penetration. Total treatment duration is 3-6 months depending on the protocol and risk group. Tumor lysis syndrome prophylaxis — IV hydration, allopurinol or rasburicase, electrolyte monitoring every 6-8 hours for the first 48 hours of chemotherapy — is mandatory. High-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem-cell rescue is used for selected relapsed or refractory patients. Relapsed Burkitt lymphoma has a poor prognosis overall; enrollment in clinical trials of novel agents (anti-CD19 bispecifics, CAR-T) is strongly encouraged.

medicalTreatments.name: CODOX-M/IVAC (cyclophosphamide, vincristine, doxorubicin, high-dose MTX / ifosfamide, etoposide, cytarabine)
medicalTreatments.description: The internationally established intensive chemotherapy backbone for Burkitt lymphoma, delivered in alternating 2-3 week cycles over 3-4 months. Low-risk patients (stage I-II, normal LDH, resected tumor) may receive only CODOX-M cycles. High-risk patients receive the full alternating CODOX-M/IVAC course.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pediatric OS >90% at 2 years with LMB or modified CODOX-M; adult OS 65-75% with rituximab addition (Mead et al. JCO 2008)

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab (375 mg/m² IV, each cycle)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody added to each chemotherapy cycle in adult patients and increasingly in pediatric regimens outside Africa. Significantly improves OS by approximately 10-15 percentage points versus chemotherapy alone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 2-year OS improves from approximately 62% (CODOX-M/IVAC alone) to 74% (plus rituximab) in HOVON 97 randomized trial (PMID 28830786)

medicalTreatments.name: DA-EPOCH-R (dose-adjusted etoposide, prednisone, vincristine, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin plus rituximab)
medicalTreatments.description: Favored adult regimen in the US, particularly for immunodeficiency-associated Burkitt lymphoma. Delivers 6 cycles over 18 weeks with CNS-directed methotrexate. Less intensive than CODOX-M/IVAC; better tolerated in older or medically frail adults.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 2-year PFS approximately 84% in the NCI cohort of sporadic and immunodeficiency-associated Burkitt (Wilson et al. JCO 2012, PMID 22392101)

medicalTreatments.name: Intrathecal chemotherapy (methotrexate 12 mg + cytarabine 30 mg, each cycle)
medicalTreatments.description: Mandatory CNS prophylaxis and treatment for all patients. Given on each cycle regardless of baseline CSF status; additional intrathecal doses given if CNS involvement is confirmed at diagnosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces CNS relapse rate from approximately 20-30% (no IT prophylaxis) to under 5% in contemporary protocols

medicalTreatments.name: Rasburicase (0.2 mg/kg IV daily for up to 5 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Recombinant urate oxidase that rapidly degrades uric acid in tumor lysis syndrome prophylaxis and treatment. Given before or at initiation of chemotherapy in all high-burden cases. Contraindicated in G6PD deficiency.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves normal uric acid within 4 hours in over 97% of patients; superior to allopurinol for established or high-risk TLS

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose methotrexate (3-8 g/m² with leucovorin rescue)
medicalTreatments.description: A component of CODOX-M cycles; achieves therapeutic CNS concentrations and is central to CNS prophylaxis in Burkitt lymphoma. Requires inpatient administration with careful hydration, urinary alkalinization, and leucovorin rescue to prevent toxicity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Essential for CNS penetration; combined with intrathecal therapy reduces CNS relapse to below 5% in pediatric protocols

surgicalOptions.name: Diagnostic biopsy (core-needle or open)
surgicalOptions.description: Diagnostic procedure to obtain adequate tissue for histology, immunophenotyping, cytogenetics/FISH, and molecular testing. Not therapeutic; chemotherapy is the curative treatment.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Core-needle biopsy achieves diagnostic material in >90% of cases; open biopsy reserved for inaccessible sites

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical resection in isolated abdominal disease (selected cases)
surgicalOptions.description: In the sporadic variant with localized ileocecal disease, primary resection followed by chemotherapy may allow stage reduction and shorter chemotherapy in low-risk children. Not performed for unresectable or advanced disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Low-risk pediatric patients with completely resected stage I disease achieve >95% EFS with abbreviated chemotherapy in LMB studies

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency laparotomy for bowel perforation
surgicalOptions.description: Required when Burkitt lymphoma presents with acute peritonitis from bowel perforation. Surgery controls the acute abdomen; chemotherapy is initiated as soon as safely possible post-operatively.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Perioperative mortality low in experienced centers; long-term outcome depends on chemotherapy response rather than surgery

whySeeSpecialist: Burkitt lymphoma requires immediate specialist evaluation by a medical oncologist or hematologist with lymphoma expertise. Treatment must begin within 24-48 hours of diagnosis in most cases. Tumor lysis syndrome, CNS prophylaxis, and intensive supportive care demand subspecialty inpatient infrastructure. Pediatric cases should be managed in pediatric oncology centers following established protocols.
doctorSelectionGuide: Select a medical oncologist, hematologist, or pediatric oncologist at a cancer center with a lymphoma program that delivers high-volume intensive regimens (CODOX-M/IVAC, LMB, DA-EPOCH-R). CNS prophylaxis, tumor lysis monitoring, and ICU-level supportive care are required. For HIV-associated cases, an infectious disease specialist or HIV physician should co-manage antiretroviral therapy.
hospitalCriteria: Hematology and oncology department with experience in high-intensity lymphoma chemotherapy
ICU access for management of tumor lysis syndrome and septic complications
Molecular pathology lab with FISH capability for MYC, BCL2, BCL6
Intrathecal therapy capability and neuro-oncology support for CNS disease
Stem cell transplant unit for relapsed/refractory cases
keyFacilities: Comprehensive cancer centers with lymphoma subspecialty programs
Pediatric oncology centers for childhood cases
FACT-accredited stem cell transplant units for salvage therapy
Clinical trial centers for relapsed/refractory disease
preventionStrategies: Maintain effective combination antiretroviral therapy (cART) in HIV-positive patients to prevent immunodeficiency-associated Burkitt lymphoma — cART reduces risk by >90% compared with untreated HIV.
Malaria chemoprevention and bednet programs in sub-Saharan Africa reduce the cumulative B-cell activation that facilitates endemic Burkitt lymphoma; EBV vaccine trials are underway.
Minimize prolonged immunosuppression after organ transplantation where safe; use prophylactic antivirals (aciclovir, ganciclovir) to reduce EBV replication in EBV-mismatch transplants.
Ensure timely clinical evaluation of any rapidly growing jaw, abdominal, or orbital mass in a child in endemic regions — delay in biopsy results in more advanced, harder-to-treat disease.
lifestyleModifications: Adhere fully to the prescribed chemotherapy schedule — treatment delays increase relapse risk given the extreme proliferative rate.
Avoid live vaccines during and for 6 months after intensive chemotherapy — immune reconstitution is gradual and vaccine responses are impaired.
Maintain adequate nutrition during treatment; high-protein, high-calorie intake supports chemotherapy tolerance and immune recovery.
HIV-positive patients should continue cART throughout chemotherapy unless guided otherwise by the HIV specialist; rituximab-based regimens are effective regardless of CD4 count.
Report any neurological symptoms (headache, vision changes, limb weakness) immediately during or after treatment — CNS relapse can occur and is an urgent oncological emergency.
Undergo recommended follow-up CT scans at 3 and 6 months post-treatment and blood tests as scheduled to detect early relapse.

recommended: High-protein foods (lean meats, eggs, dairy, legumes) to support immune recovery and prevent chemotherapy-related muscle wasting
Small frequent meals if nausea is limiting food intake; oral nutritional supplements as needed
Good hydration — at least 2-3 L/day of fluids during chemotherapy to support tumor lysis prophylaxis and renal protection
Folate-rich foods (leafy greens, legumes) to support marrow recovery between cycles
avoid: Raw or undercooked meat, fish, and eggs during neutropenia — risk of bacterial and parasitic foodborne infections when immune system is suppressed
Unpasteurized dairy products and fresh-squeezed juices during chemotherapy-induced neutropenia
Alcohol during chemotherapy — impairs liver function, worsens methotrexate toxicity, and reduces immune recovery
Grapefruit and pomelo — inhibit CYP3A4 and increase plasma levels of several chemotherapy agents

exerciseGuidelines: Light activity is encouraged during and after treatment to prevent deconditioning and support psychological wellbeing, but intense exercise should be avoided during neutropenia or thrombocytopenia. Patients with bone marrow disease should avoid contact sports to prevent bleeding. After completing treatment and achieving remission, graduated aerobic exercise supports long-term recovery and reduces fatigue.
dailyManagement: Take temperature twice daily and report any fever above 38.0°C immediately — neutropenic fever is a medical emergency.
Monitor for signs of tumor lysis syndrome (muscle cramps, reduced urine output, confusion) in the first 48 hours of each chemotherapy cycle.
Maintain oral hygiene with gentle toothbrushing and chlorhexidine rinses to prevent oral mucositis becoming infected.
Drink prescribed volumes of oral fluid each day to protect kidneys during high-dose methotrexate cycles.
Attend all planned outpatient blood count checks — dose adjustments and cycle timing are based on counts.
Keep a written record of all medications, doses, and any side-effects to report at each clinic visit.
prognosis: Burkitt lymphoma has among the best cure rates of any aggressive lymphoma when treated with appropriate intensive regimens. Children treated in high-resource settings achieve event-free survival (EFS) above 90% at 3 years with LMB or modified CODOX-M protocols. Adults have substantially lower cure rates — 2-year OS 50-75% depending on risk factors — primarily because treatment toxicity limits chemotherapy dose intensity and because adult biology may harbor additional unfavorable molecular aberrations. Risk stratification divides patients into low risk (stage I-II, normal LDH, resected tumor; EFS >95%) and high risk (stage III-IV, CNS disease, elevated LDH; EFS 65-80%). Relapsed or refractory Burkitt lymphoma has a dismal prognosis with conventional salvage: long-term survival is below 20-30% even with autologous stem-cell transplant. Novel agents — anti-CD19 bispecific antibodies, CAR-T cell therapy — are in trials for relapsed disease. HIV-positive patients treated with cART and rituximab-containing regimens achieve outcomes comparable to HIV-negative patients in contemporary prospective studies.
recoveryTimeline: Most patients achieve complete remission after 2-3 cycles of chemotherapy (approximately 6-8 weeks). Total treatment is 3-6 months. Post-treatment CT and bone marrow confirmation of remission is typically performed 4-6 weeks after the last cycle. Follow-up with clinical review and imaging every 3 months for the first 2 years detects the majority of relapses, which occur almost exclusively within 12 months.
complications: Tumor lysis syndrome — the most common metabolic emergency at treatment initiation; hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, and acute kidney injury can develop within hours of starting chemotherapy without adequate prophylaxis.

Febrile neutropenia — profound neutropenia between chemotherapy cycles increases susceptibility to gram-negative sepsis, fungal infections, and Pneumocystis pneumonia; requires immediate IV broad-spectrum antibiotics.
CNS relapse — despite intrathecal prophylaxis, CNS relapse occurs in 5-10% of high-risk patients; treatment is challenging and prognosis is poor.

Anthracycline-related cardiotoxicity — cumulative doxorubicin doses above 250-300 mg/m² increase lifetime left-ventricular dysfunction risk; cardiac monitoring and angiotensin pathway agents are used preventively.
Secondary malignancy — alkylating agents (cyclophosphamide, ifosfamide) increase risk of secondary myelodysplastic syndrome and acute myeloid leukemia; incidence approximately 1-2% at 10 years.

Growth and fertility impairment in children — alkylating agents and cranial radiation (now rarely used) can impair linear growth and gonadal function; fertility preservation counseling before treatment is standard of care for postpubertal patients.

supportResources.name: Lymphoma Research Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient education, support groups, and clinical trial listings for all lymphoma types including Burkitt lymphoma.

supportResources.name: National Cancer Institute — Burkitt Lymphoma
supportResources.description: NCI PDQ cancer information on NHL subtypes including Burkitt lymphoma, staging, and treatment options.

supportResources.name: Leukemia & Lymphoma Society (LLS)
supportResources.description: Patient support, financial assistance resources, and education on lymphoma including high-grade subtypes.

supportResources.name: Children's Oncology Group (COG)
supportResources.description: Information on pediatric cancer clinical trials and treatment guidelines for children with Burkitt lymphoma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Other non-Hodgkin lymphoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Other aggressive B-cell NHLs (diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, double-hit lymphoma) can mimic Burkitt histologically. Burkitt lymphoma is distinguished by 100% Ki-67 proliferative index, CD20+/BCL2-negative immunophenotype, and isolated MYC translocation without BCL2/BCL6 co-rearrangement on FISH. Double-hit lymphoma has co-rearrangement and a worse prognosis requiring different treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (L3 type)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Burkitt leukemia (FAB L3 ALL) is the leukemic phase of Burkitt lymphoma with >25% circulating tumor cells. It shares identical molecular pathology. Treatment is the same as Burkitt lymphoma, not standard ALL therapy. Distinguished from B-ALL by surface immunoglobulin expression, MYC translocation, and absence of TdT.

confusedWithConditions.name: HIV-related infection or other AIDS-defining illness
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: In HIV-positive patients, rapidly growing lymph nodes may be mistaken for reactive lymphadenopathy, tuberculosis, or Kaposi sarcoma. Burkitt lymphoma is distinguished by the extreme growth rate, B symptoms, highly elevated LDH, and definitive tissue biopsy. All enlarging lymph nodes in HIV-positive patients with systemic symptoms should be biopsied.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Burkitt lymphoma is a subtype of high-grade non-Hodgkin lymphoma; treatment protocols overlap at specialized lymphoma centers.

relatedConditions.name: Leukemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Burkitt leukemia (L3-ALL) is the leukemic phase of Burkitt lymphoma and requires Burkitt-specific chemotherapy rather than standard ALL regimens.

relatedConditions.name: Angioimmunoblastic T-cell lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Another aggressive lymphoma subtype managed by hematologic oncologists; distinguished from Burkitt by T-cell origin, nodal predominance, and different molecular pathology and treatment.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: HIV is a major risk factor for immunodeficiency-associated Burkitt lymphoma; cART significantly reduces risk and improves treatment outcomes.

faqs.question: What is Burkitt lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Burkitt lymphoma is one of the fastest-growing human cancers — a highly aggressive B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma caused by a chromosomal translocation that constitutively activates the MYC oncogene, driving near-universal cell-cycle entry. Despite its extreme growth rate, it is highly curable with intensive chemotherapy: event-free survival exceeds 90% in children and 65-75% in adults.

faqs.question: What are the three types of Burkitt lymphoma?
faqs.answer: The three types are: endemic (African) Burkitt lymphoma, affecting children in the malaria belt of sub-Saharan Africa with a jaw or orbital tumor; sporadic Burkitt lymphoma, presenting as an abdominal mass outside endemic regions; and immunodeficiency-associated Burkitt lymphoma, occurring in HIV-positive patients or transplant recipients.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of Burkitt lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Endemic disease presents with a rapidly enlarging jaw or facial mass. Sporadic disease most commonly causes an abdominal mass with pain, vomiting, or obstruction. Both can cause B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss). CNS involvement causes cranial nerve palsies or headache in 13-17% of patients at diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is Burkitt lymphoma curable?
faqs.answer: Yes. With modern intensive chemotherapy, event-free survival exceeds 90% in children and 65-75% in adults. Rituximab added to chemotherapy improved adult outcomes by approximately 10-15 percentage points. Relapsed Burkitt lymphoma has a much worse prognosis, making prompt first-line treatment critical.

faqs.question: How fast does Burkitt lymphoma grow?
faqs.answer: Burkitt lymphoma has a tumor doubling time of approximately 24-48 hours — making it the fastest-growing human tumor. The Ki-67 proliferative index is close to 100% on histology. This growth rate explains why treatment must begin within 24-48 hours of diagnosis and why delays significantly worsen outcomes.

faqs.question: What chemotherapy is used for Burkitt lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Standard regimens include CODOX-M/IVAC (cyclophosphamide, vincristine, doxorubicin, high-dose methotrexate alternating with ifosfamide, etoposide, cytarabine) and DA-EPOCH-R. Rituximab is added to adult regimens. Pediatric centers use LMB protocols. Treatment lasts 3-6 months; CNS prophylaxis with intrathecal chemotherapy is mandatory in all patients.

faqs.question: What is tumor lysis syndrome in Burkitt lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Tumor lysis syndrome occurs when rapid cancer cell death at treatment initiation releases potassium, phosphate, and uric acid into the bloodstream, causing dangerous electrolyte imbalances and acute kidney injury. All Burkitt lymphoma patients require IV hydration and rasburicase or allopurinol before chemotherapy, with electrolyte monitoring every 6-8 hours for the first 48 hours.

faqs.question: Does HIV increase the risk of Burkitt lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Yes. HIV increases Burkitt lymphoma risk approximately 200-1,000-fold compared with the general population. Combination antiretroviral therapy (cART) reduces risk substantially. Unlike other AIDS-defining lymphomas, Burkitt lymphoma can occur at relatively preserved CD4 counts (above 200 cells/µL) in HIV-positive patients.

faqs.question: What is the MYC translocation in Burkitt lymphoma?
faqs.answer: The MYC translocation t(8;14)(q24;q32) moves the MYC oncogene on chromosome 8 next to the immunoglobulin heavy-chain enhancer on chromosome 14, switching MYC on permanently. This drives unregulated cell proliferation and is the defining molecular event in all three Burkitt lymphoma variants. It is confirmed by FISH on biopsy tissue.

faqs.question: Can Burkitt lymphoma affect the brain?
faqs.answer: Yes. CNS involvement occurs at diagnosis in 13-17% of patients and can cause cranial nerve palsies, headache from meningeal infiltration, or spinal cord compression. Intrathecal chemotherapy given during every cycle of systemic treatment is mandatory for CNS prophylaxis; high-dose methotrexate also penetrates the CNS.

faqs.question: How is Burkitt lymphoma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires core-needle or surgical biopsy showing medium-sized B-cells with 100% Ki-67, a starry sky appearance, CD20+/BCL2-negative immunophenotype, and MYC translocation confirmed by FISH. Staging uses CT-PET, bone marrow biopsy, and lumbar puncture. LDH is markedly elevated in almost all cases.

faqs.question: Is Burkitt lymphoma common in children?
faqs.answer: Yes. Burkitt lymphoma is the most common pediatric lymphoma in sub-Saharan Africa, accounting for 30-50% of childhood lymphomas in holoendemic malaria regions. In high-income countries it is less common but still one of the major aggressive pediatric lymphomas. Outcomes in children are excellent with modern protocols.

faqs.question: What happens if Burkitt lymphoma relapses?
faqs.answer: Relapsed Burkitt lymphoma has a poor prognosis, with long-term survival below 20-30% even with intensive salvage chemotherapy and autologous stem-cell transplant. Enrollment in clinical trials testing novel agents such as anti-CD19 bispecific antibodies and CAR-T cell therapy is strongly recommended for relapsed or refractory disease.

faqs.question: What does a Burkitt lymphoma jaw tumor look like?
faqs.answer: The endemic variant produces a rapidly enlarging, hard, painless swelling of the jaw — most commonly the posterior mandible adjacent to erupting molar teeth in children aged 5-10 years. The mass can distort the entire lower face within days. Unlike dental abscess, it is not fluctuant and grows faster than any dental or infectious cause.

faqs.question: Why is Burkitt lymphoma associated with malaria?
faqs.answer: Holoendemic Plasmodium falciparum malaria drives intense and repeated polyclonal B-cell activation and impairs EBV-specific T-cell surveillance. This creates the conditions in which EBV-infected B cells can accumulate the MYC chromosomal translocation and escape immune control. The geographic distribution of endemic Burkitt lymphoma mirrors the malaria belt precisely.
